instruction: Build a C++ log parser using std::regex with named groups, comparing performance against RE2 and Hyperscan alternatives, and demonstrating when each library is the right choice.
output:
<think>
C++ has multiple regex options:
1. std::regex (C++11): Standard, portable, but notoriously slow (especially with complex patterns)
2. RE2 (Google): Linear time guarantee, no backtracking, fast for most patterns
3. Hyperscan (Intel): SIMD-accelerated, designed for multi-pattern scanning, fastest for network/log analysis
4. PCRE2: Full Perl-compatible regex, fast with JIT compilation

std::regex is fine for simple patterns in non-hot paths. For log parsing at scale, RE2 or Hyperscan are necessary. Hyperscan excels at scanning for many patterns simultaneously.

I'll implement the same log parser with std::regex and show the RE2 equivalent, then discuss when to use each. Named groups in std::regex use the standard `(?<name>...)` syntax (C++20 may not support them well - actually std::regex doesn't support named groups in most implementations). I'll use positional captures for std::regex and named for RE2.

Key insight: std::regex with ECMAScript grammar can be 100-1000x slower than RE2 for complex patterns due to backtracking.
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <regex>
#include <string>
#include <vector>
#include <chrono>
#include <unordered_map>

struct LogEntry {
    std::string timestamp;
    std::string level;
    std::string source;
    std::string message;
    std::string format;
};

// === std::regex Implementation ===
// Note: std::regex does NOT support named groups in most implementations.
// We use positional captures with documented group indices.

class StdRegexParser {
    // Group indices: 1=timestamp, 2=host, 3=service, 4=pid, 5=message
    std::regex syslog_re_{
        R"(^(\w{3}\s+\d{1,2}\s+\d{2}:\d{2}:\d{2})\s+(\S+)\s+(\w+)(?:\[(\d+)\])?:\s+(.+)$)"
    };
    // Group: 1=timestamp, 2=level, 3=source, 4=message
    std::regex bracket_re_{
        R"(^\[([^\]]+)\]\s+(DEBUG|INFO|WARN(?:ING)?|ERROR|FATAL|CRITICAL)\s+(?:in\s+)?([\w.:]+):\s+(.+)$)",
        std::regex::icase
    };
    // Key=value pairs: 1=key, 2=quoted_val, 3=unquoted_val
    std::regex kv_re_{R"((\w+)=(?:"([^"]*)"|([^\s,]+)))"};

public:
    std::optional<LogEntry> parse(const std::string& line) {
        std::smatch m;

        if (std::regex_match(line, m, syslog_re_)) {
            return LogEntry{m[1], "INFO", m[3], m[5], "syslog"};
        }
        if (std::regex_match(line, m, bracket_re_)) {
            std::string level = m[2];
            std::transform(level.begin(), level.end(), level.begin(), ::toupper);
            return LogEntry{m[1], level, m[3], m[4], "bracket"};
        }

        // Key-value format
        std::unordered_map<std::string, std::string> kv;
        auto begin = std::sregex_iterator(line.begin(), line.end(), kv_re_);
        auto end = std::sregex_iterator();
        for (auto it = begin; it != end; ++it) {
            std::string key = (*it)[1];
            std::string val = (*it)[2].matched ? (*it)[2].str() : (*it)[3].str();
            kv[key] = val;
        }
        if (!kv.empty()) {
            return LogEntry{
                kv.count("ts") ? kv["ts"] : kv["timestamp"],
                kv.count("level") ? kv["level"] : "INFO",
                kv.count("source") ? kv["source"] : "",
                kv.count("msg") ? kv["msg"] : kv["message"],
                "keyval"
            };
        }
        return std::nullopt;
    }
};

// === Performance comparison framework ===
class BenchmarkRunner {
public:
    template<typename Func>
    static double measure(const std::string& name, int iterations, Func&& func) {
        auto start = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
        for (int i = 0; i < iterations; ++i) {
            func();
        }
        auto end = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
        auto us = std::chrono::duration_cast<std::chrono::microseconds>(end - start).count();
        double per_op = static_cast<double>(us) / iterations;
        std::cout << name << ": " << us << "us total, "
                  << per_op << "us/op (" << iterations << " iters)\n";
        return per_op;
    }
};

/*
// === RE2 Implementation (requires google/re2) ===
// RE2 supports named groups: (?P<name>pattern)
//
// #include <re2/re2.h>
//
// static const re2::RE2 syslog_re(
//     R"(^(?P<ts>\w{3}\s+\d{1,2}\s+\d{2}:\d{2}:\d{2})\s+(?P<host>\S+)\s+(?P<svc>\w+)(?:\[(?P<pid>\d+)\])?:\s+(?P<msg>.+)$)"
// );
//
// std::string ts, host, svc, msg;
// if (RE2::FullMatch(line, syslog_re, &ts, &host, &svc, nullptr, &msg)) {
//     // 10-100x faster than std::regex for complex patterns
// }
//
// === Hyperscan Implementation (requires intel/hyperscan) ===
// Best for: scanning many patterns simultaneously against a stream
// Compile patterns into a database, scan once, get all matches.
// Ideal for: IDS/IPS, log classification, content filtering.
*/

// === When to use each library ===
void printGuide() {
    std::cout << R"(
=== C++ Regex Library Selection Guide ===

  std::regex:
    + Portable, no dependencies
    + Fine for config parsing, one-off matches
    - VERY slow (100-1000x vs RE2)
    - No named groups in most implementations
    - Avoid in hot paths or large input

  RE2 (google/re2):
    + Linear time guarantee (no catastrophic backtracking)
    + Named groups: (?P<name>...)
    + 10-100x faster than std::regex
    + Good for log parsing, input validation
    - No backreferences or lookaround
    - Single-pattern at a time

  Hyperscan (intel/hyperscan):
    + SIMD-accelerated, fastest option
    + Multi-pattern scanning in single pass
    + Streaming mode for network data
    + Ideal for IDS, log classification
    - Intel x86 only (no ARM)
    - More complex API
    - No capture groups (match/no-match only)

  PCRE2:
    + Full Perl-compatible (lookaround, backrefs)
    + JIT compilation for speed
    + Named groups, recursive patterns
    - Not standard C++
    - Can catastrophically backtrack
)";
}

int main() {
    StdRegexParser parser;
    std::vector<std::string> lines = {
        "Mar 15 10:30:45 web01 nginx[1234]: GET /api/users 200",
        "[2024-03-15T10:31:00Z] ERROR auth.service: token validation failed",
        R"(ts=2024-03-15 level=warn source=db msg="pool exhausted")",
    };

    for (const auto& line : lines) {
        if (auto entry = parser.parse(line)) {
            std::cout << "[" << entry->format << "] "
                      << entry->level << ": " << entry->message << "\n";
        }
    }

    // Benchmark
    const int N = 10000;
    BenchmarkRunner::measure("std::regex parse", N, [&]() {
        for (const auto& line : lines) parser.parse(line);
    });

    printGuide();
}
```

instruction: Implement a C++ compile-time regex validator using constexpr and template metaprogramming that validates regex patterns at compile time, with a runtime fallback for dynamic patterns.
output:
<think>
Compile-time regex validation in C++ is an advanced technique. The idea: validate regex pattern syntax at compile time using constexpr functions, so invalid patterns cause compilation errors rather than runtime exceptions.

C++20 constexpr is powerful enough to do string processing at compile time. I can write a constexpr function that parses a regex pattern and returns whether it's valid. If used in a static_assert, invalid patterns fail to compile.

For the implementation:
1. constexpr regex pattern validator that checks: balanced brackets, valid escape sequences, valid quantifiers, etc.
2. A compile-time string wrapper (since string literals aren't directly usable as template args before C++20)
3. Runtime fallback using std::regex for dynamic patterns
4. A unified interface that uses compile-time validation when possible

I'll use C++20 features: constexpr std::string_view, consteval for forced compile-time evaluation.
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <regex>
#include <string_view>
#include <stdexcept>
#include <optional>

// === Compile-time regex pattern validator ===

struct ValidationResult {
    bool valid;
    int error_pos;        // -1 if valid
    const char* message;  // error description

    constexpr operator bool() const { return valid; }
};

constexpr ValidationResult regex_ok() {
    return {true, -1, "ok"};
}

constexpr ValidationResult regex_error(int pos, const char* msg) {
    return {false, pos, msg};
}

// Constexpr regex pattern validator
constexpr ValidationResult validate_regex_pattern(std::string_view pattern) {
    int paren_depth = 0;
    int bracket_depth = 0;
    int brace_depth = 0;
    bool in_charset = false;
    bool escaped = false;

    for (int i = 0; i < static_cast<int>(pattern.size()); ++i) {
        char c = pattern[i];

        if (escaped) {
            escaped = false;
            // Validate escape sequences
            if (c == 'd' || c == 'D' || c == 'w' || c == 'W' ||
                c == 's' || c == 'S' || c == 'b' || c == 'B' ||
                c == 'n' || c == 'r' || c == 't' || c == 'f' ||
                c == '.' || c == '*' || c == '+' || c == '?' ||
                c == '(' || c == ')' || c == '[' || c == ']' ||
                c == '{' || c == '}' || c == '\\' || c == '|' ||
                c == '^' || c == '$' || c == '/' ||
                (c >= '0' && c <= '9')) {
                continue;
            }
            return regex_error(i, "Invalid escape sequence");
        }

        if (c == '\\') {
            escaped = true;
            continue;
        }

        if (in_charset) {
            if (c == ']') { in_charset = false; bracket_depth--; }
            continue;
        }

        switch (c) {
            case '[':
                in_charset = true;
                bracket_depth++;
                break;
            case ']':
                return regex_error(i, "Unmatched ]");
            case '(':
                paren_depth++;
                // Check for valid group syntax
                if (i + 1 < static_cast<int>(pattern.size()) && pattern[i + 1] == '?') {
                    if (i + 2 >= static_cast<int>(pattern.size())) {
                        return regex_error(i, "Incomplete group syntax");
                    }
                    char gc = pattern[i + 2];
                    if (gc != ':' && gc != '=' && gc != '!' && gc != '<' &&
                        gc != 'P' && gc != 'i' && gc != 'm' && gc != 's') {
                        return regex_error(i + 2, "Invalid group modifier");
                    }
                }
                break;
            case ')':
                paren_depth--;
                if (paren_depth < 0) return regex_error(i, "Unmatched )");
                break;
            case '{':
                brace_depth++;
                break;
            case '}':
                brace_depth--;
                if (brace_depth < 0) return regex_error(i, "Unmatched }");
                break;
            case '*': case '+': case '?':
                // Quantifier must follow something quantifiable
                if (i == 0 || pattern[i - 1] == '(' || pattern[i - 1] == '|') {
                    return regex_error(i, "Quantifier without preceding element");
                }
                break;
            case '|':
                // Alternation - valid almost anywhere
                break;
        }
    }

    if (escaped) return regex_error(static_cast<int>(pattern.size()) - 1, "Trailing backslash");
    if (paren_depth != 0) return regex_error(-1, "Unmatched (");
    if (in_charset) return regex_error(-1, "Unmatched [");
    if (brace_depth != 0) return regex_error(-1, "Unmatched {");

    return regex_ok();
}

// === Compile-time validated regex wrapper ===

template<size_t N>
struct StaticString {
    char data[N];
    consteval StaticString(const char (&str)[N]) {
        for (size_t i = 0; i < N; ++i) data[i] = str[i];
    }
    consteval std::string_view view() const { return {data, N - 1}; }
};

// Compile-time validated regex: invalid patterns fail to compile
template<StaticString Pattern>
class StaticRegex {
    static constexpr auto validation = validate_regex_pattern(Pattern.view());
    static_assert(validation.valid, "Invalid regex pattern");

    std::regex re_;
public:
    StaticRegex() : re_(Pattern.data) {}

    bool match(const std::string& text) const {
        return std::regex_match(text, re_);
    }
    bool search(const std::string& text) const {
        return std::regex_search(text, re_);
    }
    std::optional<std::smatch> find(const std::string& text) const {
        std::smatch m;
        if (std::regex_search(text, m, re_)) return m;
        return std::nullopt;
    }
};

// Runtime regex with validation
class DynamicRegex {
    std::regex re_;
    std::string pattern_;
public:
    static ValidationResult validate(std::string_view pattern) {
        return validate_regex_pattern(pattern);
    }

    explicit DynamicRegex(const std::string& pattern) : pattern_(pattern) {
        auto result = validate(pattern);
        if (!result) {
            throw std::invalid_argument(
                std::string("Invalid regex at position ") +
                std::to_string(result.error_pos) + ": " + result.message
            );
        }
        re_ = std::regex(pattern);
    }

    bool match(const std::string& text) const {
        return std::regex_match(text, re_);
    }
};

int main() {
    // Compile-time validated: invalid patterns won't compile
    StaticRegex<R"(\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2})"> date_re;
    StaticRegex<R"([\w.]+@[\w.]+)"> email_re;

    // These would fail to compile:
    // StaticRegex<R"([unclosed)"> bad1;  // Unmatched (
    // StaticRegex<R"(*invalid)"> bad2;   // Quantifier without element

    std::cout << std::boolalpha;
    std::cout << "Date match: " << date_re.match("2024-03-15") << '\n'; // true
    std::cout << "Email search: " << email_re.search("hi user@example.com") << '\n'; // true

    // Runtime validation for user-supplied patterns
    try {
        std::string user_pattern = "[valid]+";
        DynamicRegex dyn(user_pattern);
        std::cout << "Dynamic: " << dyn.match("valid") << '\n';
    } catch (const std::exception& e) {
        std::cerr << "Error: " << e.what() << '\n';
    }

    // Validation API
    auto r = DynamicRegex::validate("(unclosed");
    if (!r) std::cout << "Validation: " << r.message << " at " << r.error_pos << '\n';
}
```

instruction: Create a C++ high-performance string search system that compares std::regex, std::string::find, and manual Boyer-Moore for pattern matching, showing when regex is appropriate vs simpler alternatives.
output:
<think>
This is about choosing the right string matching tool in C++. The three approaches:

1. std::string::find: Simple substring search. O(n*m) worst case but fast in practice. Best for literal substring matching.
2. std::regex: Most flexible, supports complex patterns. But very slow compared to alternatives. Best for complex pattern matching in non-critical paths.
3. Boyer-Moore: Fast algorithm for literal string search. O(n/m) best case (skips characters). Best for searching long patterns in large texts.
4. std::search with boyer_moore_searcher (C++17): STL-provided Boyer-Moore.

I'll implement all three, benchmark them, and show decision guidelines.
</think>

```cpp
#include <algorithm>
#include <chrono>
#include <functional>
#include <iostream>
#include <regex>
#include <string>
#include <vector>

// === Boyer-Moore implementation ===
class BoyerMoore {
    std::string pattern_;
    int bad_char_[256];
    std::vector<int> good_suffix_;

public:
    explicit BoyerMoore(const std::string& pattern) : pattern_(pattern) {
        int m = static_cast<int>(pattern_.size());
        // Bad character table
        std::fill(std::begin(bad_char_), std::end(bad_char_), -1);
        for (int i = 0; i < m; ++i) {
            bad_char_[static_cast<unsigned char>(pattern_[i])] = i;
        }
        // Good suffix table (simplified)
        good_suffix_.resize(m + 1, m);
        std::vector<int> suffix(m + 1, 0);
        int g = m, f = 0;
        for (int i = m - 1; i >= 0; --i) {
            if (i > g && suffix[i + m - 1 - f] < i - g) {
                suffix[i] = suffix[i + m - 1 - f];
            } else {
                if (i < g) g = i;
                f = i;
                while (g >= 0 && pattern_[g] == pattern_[g + m - 1 - f]) --g;
                suffix[i] = f - g;
            }
        }
        for (int i = 0; i < m; ++i) good_suffix_[i] = m;
        for (int i = m - 1; i >= 0; --i) {
            if (suffix[i] == i + 1) {
                for (int j = 0; j < m - 1 - i; ++j) {
                    if (good_suffix_[j] == m) good_suffix_[j] = m - 1 - i;
                }
            }
        }
        for (int i = 0; i < m - 1; ++i) {
            good_suffix_[m - 1 - suffix[i]] = m - 1 - i;
        }
    }

    int search(const std::string& text) const {
        int n = static_cast<int>(text.size());
        int m = static_cast<int>(pattern_.size());
        if (m == 0) return 0;
        int i = 0;
        while (i <= n - m) {
            int j = m - 1;
            while (j >= 0 && pattern_[j] == text[i + j]) --j;
            if (j < 0) return i;
            int bc_shift = j - bad_char_[static_cast<unsigned char>(text[i + j])];
            int gs_shift = good_suffix_[j];
            i += std::max(bc_shift, gs_shift);
        }
        return -1;  // Not found
    }

    std::vector<int> search_all(const std::string& text) const {
        std::vector<int> positions;
        int n = static_cast<int>(text.size());
        int m = static_cast<int>(pattern_.size());
        if (m == 0) return positions;
        int i = 0;
        while (i <= n - m) {
            int j = m - 1;
            while (j >= 0 && pattern_[j] == text[i + j]) --j;
            if (j < 0) {
                positions.push_back(i);
                i += good_suffix_[0];
            } else {
                i += std::max(j - bad_char_[static_cast<unsigned char>(text[i + j])],
                              good_suffix_[j]);
            }
        }
        return positions;
    }
};

// === Benchmark harness ===
template<typename Func>
double benchmark(const std::string& name, int iterations, Func&& fn) {
    auto start = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    int result = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < iterations; ++i) {
        result += fn() ? 1 : 0;
    }
    auto end = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    auto ns = std::chrono::duration_cast<std::chrono::nanoseconds>(end - start).count();
    double per_op = static_cast<double>(ns) / iterations;
    std::cout << name << ": " << per_op << " ns/op"
              << " (" << result << "/" << iterations << " matched)\n";
    return per_op;
}

int main() {
    // Generate test data
    std::string text(100000, 'a');
    text += "needle_in_haystack";
    text += std::string(100000, 'b');
    const std::string pattern = "needle_in_haystack";
    const int N = 1000;

    std::cout << "=== Literal Pattern Search (" << text.size() << " chars) ===\n\n";

    // 1. std::string::find
    double t1 = benchmark("string::find      ", N, [&]() {
        return text.find(pattern) != std::string::npos;
    });

    // 2. Boyer-Moore (manual)
    BoyerMoore bm(pattern);
    double t2 = benchmark("Boyer-Moore       ", N, [&]() {
        return bm.search(text) >= 0;
    });

    // 3. std::search with BM searcher (C++17)
    auto searcher = std::boyer_moore_searcher(pattern.begin(), pattern.end());
    double t3 = benchmark("std::search(BM)   ", N, [&]() {
        return std::search(text.begin(), text.end(), searcher) != text.end();
    });

    // 4. std::regex
    std::regex re(pattern);
    double t4 = benchmark("std::regex        ", N, [&]() {
        return std::regex_search(text, re);
    });

    std::cout << "\n=== Relative Performance ===\n";
    std::cout << "string::find:    1.0x (baseline)\n";
    std::cout << "Boyer-Moore:     " << t1 / t2 << "x\n";
    std::cout << "std::search(BM): " << t1 / t3 << "x\n";
    std::cout << "std::regex:      " << t1 / t4 << "x (" << (t4 > t1 ? "slower" : "faster") << ")\n";

    std::cout << R"(
=== Decision Guide ===
  Use string::find when:
    - Searching for a literal substring
    - Pattern is fixed at compile time
    - Simple and fast enough for most cases

  Use Boyer-Moore / std::search when:
    - Long patterns (>10 chars) in very large texts
    - Pattern is known ahead of time (amortize setup)
    - Need every occurrence in large corpus

  Use std::regex when:
    - Pattern has wildcards, alternation, quantifiers
    - Pattern comes from user input / config
    - NOT in a hot loop (10-1000x slower)
    - Prefer RE2 for performance-critical regex
)";
}
```

instruction: Implement a C++ regex-based CSV parser that handles quoted fields, embedded commas, embedded newlines, and escape sequences, comparing std::regex with a hand-rolled state machine parser for correctness and performance.
output:
<think>
CSV parsing is a classic case where regex is tempting but problematic. A proper CSV parser needs to handle:
1. Quoted fields: `"hello, world"` - comma inside quotes is not a delimiter
2. Embedded quotes: `"he said ""hello"""` - doubled quotes inside quoted fields
3. Embedded newlines: `"line1\nline2"` - newline inside quoted field
4. Mixed: some fields quoted, some not
5. Trailing commas, empty fields

Regex approach: possible but fragile. The pattern for a single field is complex: `("(?:[^"]|"")*"|[^,\n]*)`. For multiline, need DOTALL.

State machine approach: simple, fast, correct. States: START, IN_FIELD, IN_QUOTED, QUOTE_IN_QUOTED. Process character by character.

I'll implement both and compare. The state machine will be significantly faster and handle edge cases better.
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <regex>
#include <sstream>
#include <string>
#include <vector>
#include <chrono>
#include <cassert>

using Row = std::vector<std::string>;
using Table = std::vector<Row>;

// === State Machine CSV Parser (recommended) ===
class StateMachineParser {
    enum State { START, UNQUOTED, QUOTED, QUOTE_END };
public:
    Table parse(const std::string& input) {
        Table table;
        Row currentRow;
        std::string currentField;
        State state = START;

        for (size_t i = 0; i <= input.size(); ++i) {
            char c = (i < input.size()) ? input[i] : '\0'; // Sentinel

            switch (state) {
            case START:
                if (c == '"') {
                    state = QUOTED;
                } else if (c == ',') {
                    currentRow.push_back(currentField);
                    currentField.clear();
                } else if (c == '\n' || c == '\0') {
                    currentRow.push_back(currentField);
                    currentField.clear();
                    if (!currentRow.empty() &&
                        !(currentRow.size() == 1 && currentRow[0].empty())) {
                        table.push_back(std::move(currentRow));
                    }
                    currentRow = Row{};
                } else if (c == '\r') {
                    // Skip CR
                } else {
                    currentField += c;
                    state = UNQUOTED;
                }
                break;

            case UNQUOTED:
                if (c == ',') {
                    currentRow.push_back(currentField);
                    currentField.clear();
                    state = START;
                } else if (c == '\n' || c == '\0') {
                    currentRow.push_back(currentField);
                    currentField.clear();
                    table.push_back(std::move(currentRow));
                    currentRow = Row{};
                    state = START;
                } else if (c == '\r') {
                    // Skip
                } else {
                    currentField += c;
                }
                break;

            case QUOTED:
                if (c == '"') {
                    state = QUOTE_END;
                } else if (c == '\0') {
                    // Unterminated quote - best effort
                    currentRow.push_back(currentField);
                    table.push_back(std::move(currentRow));
                    return table;
                } else {
                    currentField += c; // Newlines and commas are literal
                }
                break;

            case QUOTE_END:
                if (c == '"') {
                    // Escaped quote
                    currentField += '"';
                    state = QUOTED;
                } else if (c == ',') {
                    currentRow.push_back(currentField);
                    currentField.clear();
                    state = START;
                } else if (c == '\n' || c == '\0') {
                    currentRow.push_back(currentField);
                    currentField.clear();
                    table.push_back(std::move(currentRow));
                    currentRow = Row{};
                    state = START;
                } else {
                    currentField += c; // Garbage after close quote
                    state = UNQUOTED;
                }
                break;
            }
        }
        return table;
    }
};

// === Regex CSV Parser (for comparison) ===
class RegexParser {
    // Field: quoted field with escaped quotes, or unquoted field
    std::regex field_re_{R"("((?:[^"]|"")*)"|([^,\n]*))"};
    std::regex row_re_{R"([^\n]*)"};
public:
    Table parse(const std::string& input) {
        Table table;
        // Simple line-by-line (breaks on embedded newlines in quotes)
        std::istringstream stream(input);
        std::string line;
        while (std::getline(stream, line)) {
            if (line.empty()) continue;
            Row row;
            auto begin = std::sregex_iterator(line.begin(), line.end(), field_re_);
            auto end = std::sregex_iterator();
            for (auto it = begin; it != end; ++it) {
                if ((*it)[1].matched) {
                    // Quoted: unescape doubled quotes
                    std::string val = (*it)[1].str();
                    std::string unescaped;
                    for (size_t i = 0; i < val.size(); ++i) {
                        if (val[i] == '"' && i + 1 < val.size() && val[i + 1] == '"') {
                            unescaped += '"';
                            ++i;
                        } else {
                            unescaped += val[i];
                        }
                    }
                    row.push_back(unescaped);
                } else {
                    row.push_back((*it)[2].str());
                }
            }
            if (!row.empty()) table.push_back(std::move(row));
        }
        return table;
    }
};

void printTable(const Table& table) {
    for (size_t r = 0; r < table.size(); ++r) {
        std::cout << "Row " << r << ": ";
        for (size_t c = 0; c < table[r].size(); ++c) {
            if (c > 0) std::cout << " | ";
            std::cout << "[" << table[r][c] << "]";
        }
        std::cout << "\n";
    }
}

int main() {
    std::string csv = R"(name,age,bio
Alice,30,"Software engineer, loves C++"
Bob,25,"Said ""hello"" to everyone"
Carol,28,"Multi
line
bio here"
)";

    std::cout << "=== State Machine Parser ===\n";
    StateMachineParser sm;
    auto table1 = sm.parse(csv);
    printTable(table1);

    std::cout << "\n=== Regex Parser (note: breaks on embedded newlines) ===\n";
    RegexParser rp;
    auto table2 = rp.parse(csv);
    printTable(table2);

    // Benchmark
    std::string big_csv;
    for (int i = 0; i < 10000; ++i) {
        big_csv += "field1,";
        big_csv += "\"quoted, field\"";
        big_csv += ",field3,";
        big_csv += "\"said \"\"hi\"\"\"";
        big_csv += "\n";
    }

    auto t1 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    auto r1 = sm.parse(big_csv);
    auto t2 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();
    auto r2 = rp.parse(big_csv);
    auto t3 = std::chrono::high_resolution_clock::now();

    auto sm_us = std::chrono::duration_cast<std::chrono::microseconds>(t2 - t1).count();
    auto re_us = std::chrono::duration_cast<std::chrono::microseconds>(t3 - t2).count();

    std::cout << "\n=== Benchmark (10K rows) ===\n";
    std::cout << "State machine: " << sm_us << " us (" << r1.size() << " rows)\n";
    std::cout << "Regex:         " << re_us << " us (" << r2.size() << " rows)\n";
    std::cout << "Speedup:       " << static_cast<double>(re_us) / sm_us << "x\n";

    std::cout << "\nVerdict: State machine handles ALL edge cases and is faster.\n";
    std::cout << "Use regex only for quick prototyping, never for production CSV parsing.\n";
}
```
